metadata.summary: Production LLM cost optimization: response caching, prompt compression, async batching, model routing. Cut costs 50-80% without quality loss.

content.title: Cost Reduction Strategies
content.summary: Production LLM cost optimization through four orthogonal techniques: response caching (SHA-256 content hash key, Redis or in-memory TTL), prompt compression (whitespace normalization, redundant phrase removal), async batching, and model routing (default cheap model with fallback). The `CostOptimizedLLM` class composes all four into a single production-ready service layer.

why.p: LLM API cost scales linearly with request volume. At >1000 calls/day the bill becomes significant, and most workloads have substantial caching potential (deterministic outputs at temperature=0). Combining caching, compression, and routing can cut costs 50-80% without degrading output quality for the right task types.

list.item: Production LLM apps with high request volume (>1000 calls/day) where API costs are significant
Budget-constrained projects that need to scale without proportional cost increase
Multi-tenant SaaS where per-request costs must be predictable
Pipelines running batch classification, extraction, or summarization at scale
Low-volume prototypes — premature optimization adds complexity with no payoff
Tasks requiring temperature > 0 (non-deterministic outputs) — caching is ineffective
Latency-critical real-time systems where cache lookup adds unacceptable overhead
Workflows where output freshness is mandatory (live data, personalized responses)

section.name: Response Caching for LLM Cost Reduction

rule.statement: Cache only deterministic outputs: set temperature=0 as a precondition before writing to cache; never cache outputs from temperature > 0.
rule.rationale: Non-deterministic outputs at temperature > 0 produce different correct answers for the same input; caching them returns stale stochastic results.

rule.statement: Use SHA-256 of (model + messages + relevant kwargs) as the cache key, not the raw prompt string.
rule.rationale: Including model in the key prevents cross-model cache hits. Including max_tokens prevents returning truncated cached responses for requests with higher limits.

example.title: Cache key construction with SHA-256
example.note: sort_keys=True ensures dict ordering does not affect the hash.

rule.statement: Set TTL relative to content volatility: static documentation → 7 days; user queries → 1-4 hours; real-time data summaries → do not cache.
rule.rationale: A 7-day TTL on a prompt that references live prices serves stale data. A 1-hour TTL on FAQ answers misses most of the cache savings opportunity.

rule.statement: Invalidate cache entries when the model version changes — Redis TTL alone does not handle this; add model version to the cache key or flush on version deploy.
rule.rationale: Responses cached under model vX may be stylistically or factually different from vX+1; serving old responses after a model update degrades quality silently.

rule.statement: Track cache hit rate per endpoint; if hit rate is below 20%, the cache key is too specific or TTL is too short.
rule.rationale: Below 20% hit rate the cache adds latency overhead without meaningful cost savings; diagnose key specificity or TTL before scaling.

antipattern.description: Caching inside an agentic loop using session-scoped keys.
antipattern.reason: Agents in a loop must observe state changes between turns; session-scoped cache keys return stale tool-call results and cause the agent to act on outdated information.

rule.statement: Compress only user-facing inputs and system prompts; never compress few-shot examples, agent scratchpad, or chain-of-thought steps.
rule.rationale: Few-shot examples depend on precise phrasing and spacing to demonstrate the target format. Compressing them produces a model that learns from corrupted demonstrations. CoT compression breaks reasoning chains.

rule.statement: Run compression on the system prompt first, where savings are largest — a 500-token system prompt that compresses to 350 saves 150 tokens on every request.
rule.rationale: System prompt tokens are paid on every call; user message tokens are paid once per turn. System prompt optimization has higher ROI.

antipattern.description: Aggressive compression that strips semantic information from instructions (e.g., removing examples or detail from JSON schemas).
antipattern.reason: Losing constraint details produces outputs that do not match the required format; quality loss outweighs token savings.

rule.statement: Count tokens with tiktoken before sending any expensive call; fall back to a cheaper model if the prompt exceeds a budget threshold.
rule.rationale: Token count determines cost and latency; routing before the call is cheaper than paying for an oversized request and then retrying with a smaller model.

example.title: Token budget check before routing
example.code:
import tiktoken
def is_within_budget(text: str, model: str = "gpt-4o", max_tokens: int = 4000) -> bool: enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model(model) return len(enc.encode(text)) <= max_tokens
# Route by token budget before calling model = "gpt-4o" if is_within_budget(prompt, max_tokens=2000) else "gpt-4o-mini"
example.note: Rule-based routing — no LLM call needed for the routing decision itself.

rule.statement: Use Anthropic prompt caching (cache_control) for system prompts exceeding 1024 tokens — the 90% read discount is immediate with no code changes beyond adding the header.
rule.rationale: Anthropic caches up to 4 system prompt breakpoints; repeated calls reuse the cached prefix at 90% cost reduction, making this the highest-ROI optimization for Claude-based apps.

rule.statement: Use rule-based routing (token count thresholds, task type keyword matching) — never use an LLM call to decide which model to call.
rule.rationale: Routing via another LLM call is circular (costs money, adds latency) and creates a dependency that can fail. Rule-based routing is deterministic, free, and instant.

rule.statement: After implementing model routing, evaluate quality on a held-out test set — model downgrade silently degrades quality without A/B evaluation.
rule.rationale: Routing to a cheaper model saves cost only if quality is acceptable; routing without evaluation trades hidden quality loss for visible cost savings.

antipattern.description: Using an LLM to classify prompt complexity and select the routing model.
antipattern.reason: The routing call itself costs money and adds 200-500ms latency; for tasks shorter than 500 tokens the routing overhead exceeds the savings.

rule.statement: Use AsyncOpenAI with asyncio.gather to parallelize multiple independent prompts; never call completions.create sequentially in a for loop for batch work.
rule.rationale: Sequential calls at 1s latency each make batch pipelines 10-100x slower than necessary; async gather reduces wall-clock time to the latency of a single call.

example.title: Async batching with parallelization
example.code:
import asyncio from openai import AsyncOpenAI
async def batch_complete(prompts: list, model: str = "gpt-4o-mini") -> list: client = AsyncOpenAI() tasks = [ client.chat.completions.create( model=model, messages=[{"role": "user", "content": p}], ) for p in prompts ] responses = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True) return [ r.choices[0].message.content if not isinstance(r, Exception) else f"Error: {r}" for r in responses ]
example.note: return_exceptions=True prevents one API error from aborting the entire batch.

rule.statement: Use OpenAI Batch API for any pipeline that tolerates 24-hour latency (nightly reports, bulk classification, offline annotation) — it provides a 50% cost discount.
rule.rationale: Batch API queues requests for async processing; the 50% discount is OpenAI's standard pricing for offline workloads that do not require real-time response.

section.name: Best Practices and Agentic Gotchas
section.p: Best practices: Cache only at temperature=0; use content hash (SHA-256 of model + messages + kwargs) as key. Set TTL relative to content volatility: static docs → 7 days; user queries → 1-4 hours. Use Anthropic prompt caching for system prompts >1024 tokens — the 90% read discount is immediate. Apply OpenAI Batch API for any pipeline that tolerates 24h latency (nightly reports, bulk classification). Route by task complexity: pattern-match keywords to select cheap/expensive model, then evaluate quality on a held-out set. Track cache hit rate per endpoint; below 20% hit rate means the cache key is too specific or TTL too short. Never compress few-shot examples — the quality loss is disproportionate to the token saving.

Agentic gotchas: Caching inside an agentic loop can cause the agent to miss state changes between turns — use turn-scoped (not session-scoped) cache keys or disable cache for tool-call results. Model routing decisions made by an LLM are circular — use rule-based routing (token count thresholds, task type) not another LLM call. Budget guards must be checked synchronously before the LLM call, not after; async checks lead to overruns. Prompt compression applied to agent scratchpad or chain-of-thought steps breaks reasoning — only compress user-facing inputs. Semantic similarity caches (embedding-based) require a human-in-loop validation step when first deployed; false hit rate is non-obvious.

section.name: Agentic Workflow
section.p: A cost-reduction agent runs as a pre-processing layer before any LLM call: check cache → route to cheapest capable model → compress prompt → call API → write cache. Claude subagents can own the routing decision by receiving task metadata (complexity score, required capabilities) and returning a model name. The caching layer and budget guard are best implemented as Python middleware, not inside the agent itself.

Recommended subagents: `/faion` (knowledge/solo/sdd/sdd/) — audit existing LLM call sites and score each for caching eligibility and model downgrade potential. Custom cost-analysis agent — given a list of API call logs, compute per-endpoint cost breakdown and recommend optimizations.

section.name: Tools and Services

list.item: tiktoken: Count tokens for OpenAI models before sending | `pip install tiktoken` | github.com/openai/tiktoken
litellm: Unified API + cost tracking across 100+ providers | `pip install litellm` | litellm.ai
openai CLI: Batch API, usage stats, file management | `pip install openai` | platform.openai.com/docs/guides/batch
redis-cli: Inspect/flush response cache in production | Ships with Redis | redis.io
anthropic: Prompt caching (cache_control) for Claude | `pip install anthropic` | docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-caching

section.p: OpenAI Batch API: SaaS, async job ID, 50% cost discount, 24h turnaround, ideal for offline pipelines.
Anthropic Prompt Caching: SaaS, `cache_control` header, Caches up to 4 system prompt breakpoints, 90% read discount.
LiteLLM Proxy: OSS, OpenAI-compatible, Centralizes routing, cost tracking, fallbacks across providers.
Helicone: SaaS, proxy layer, Request logging, cost dashboard, caching layer.
Portkey: SaaS, gateway, Multi-provider routing, semantic cache, budget alerts.
Redis: OSS, Python client, Response cache backend, supports TTL, cluster mode.

template.purpose: ResponseCache + CachedLLMClient with Redis + memory-fallback (~60 lines)
Prompt for classifying LLM call sites as cacheable/downgradeable/batch-eligible
